title: Malware Family Distribution
spl:
index=security tag=malware
| rex field=signature "(?<family>[A-Za-z0-9]+)"
| stats count by family
| sort -count
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Malware.Malware_Attacks by Malware_Attacks.signature
| sort - count
description: Shifts toward ransomware or infostealer families prioritize IR playbooks.
value: Shifts toward ransomware or infostealer families prioritize IR playbooks.
grandmaExplanation: Shifts toward ransomware or infostealer families prioritize IR playbooks ‚Äî so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Malware CIM.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `signature` or `user` field for family tags.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Maintain `malware_family_lookup` for vendor-specific strings.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=security tag=malware
| rex field=signature "(?<family>[A-Za-z0-9]+)"
| stats count by family
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Malware Family Distribution** ‚Äî Shifts toward ransomware or infostealer families prioritize IR playbooks.

Documented **Data sources**: `signature` or `user` field for family tags. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Malware CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: security.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=security, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by family** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Malware.Malware_Attacks by Malware_Attacks.signature
| sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Malware Family Distribution** ‚Äî Shifts toward ransomware or infostealer families prioritize IR playbooks.

Documented **Data sources**: `signature` or `user` field for family tags. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Malware CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Malware.Malware_Attacks` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Pie chart (families), Treemap.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
